content.text: Agentic AI systems that retrieve, synthesize, or act on organizational data are only as reliable as the data beneath them. A data readiness assessment evaluates whether the sources an agent will consume are complete enough, fresh enough, structurally stable enough, accessible enough, and semantically rich enough to support the use case — before the team commits to building the full agent pipeline.

attrs.alt: Data readiness assessment framework table showing six dimensions (completeness, freshness, schema stability, access permissions, grounding quality, data lineage) with assessment criteria, readiness levels (ready/partial/not ready), and gate decisions (pass/conditional/block)

content.text: A data readiness assessment for agentic AI is a structured evaluation of the data sources the agent will use, across six dimensions: completeness, freshness, schema stability, access permissions, grounding quality, and lineage documentation. Each dimension is assessed against defined criteria and assigned a readiness level: Ready (proceed), Conditional (proceed with remediation plan), or Blocking (resolve before development begins). Any blocking dimension halts the project until resolved; conditional dimensions have explicit remediation milestones tracked against the development timeline.

Grounding feasibility — the ability to accurately retrieve relevant context for the agent's tasks — is a distinct sub-assessment that goes beyond raw data quality. It measures whether the data is semantically structured in a way that supports accurate retrieval. A table with high completeness and freshness can still score poorly on grounding quality if its records are stored in formats that produce poor embeddings (scanned PDFs with OCR errors, highly abbreviated internal codes without definitions, or deeply nested JSON that loses semantic structure when flattened to text).

Agentic systems fail in production far more often because of data problems than because of model capability problems. An agent reasoning over stale inventory data will recommend out-of-stock items. An agent grounding its answers on 40% null customer profiles will confabulate when the missing fields are relevant to the query. An agent whose data access permissions have not been approved through security review will be blocked by IT just before the planned go-live date.

Data readiness assessment surfaces these issues during the discovery phase, when they are cheap to resolve, rather than during system testing, when delays are expensive. It also produces the data architecture requirements for the agent's RAG pipeline: indexing strategy, chunking approach, refresh cadence, and access control design all flow from assessment findings.
Access permission gaps discovered in discovery take weeks to resolve; discovered in production, they halt operations entirely.
Assessment scores directly inform chunking strategy, refresh cadence, and whether document preprocessing (OCR, normalization) is required.
Conditional findings become a structured remediation backlog with owners, timelines, and milestone criteria that feed directly into the project plan.
A grounding quality score quantifies how much hallucination risk the RAG layer introduces, enabling honest conversation with stakeholders about output reliability.
The assessment documentation records which data was approved for use by the agent, a prerequisite for compliance review under data protection frameworks.
A thorough assessment across 5–10 data sources can take 2–4 weeks and requires access to data engineers, security teams, and data owners.
Data quality is not static; a source that passes the assessment may degrade after deployment if upstream processes change. Ongoing monitoring is required.
Grounding quality depends on the specific queries the agent will face. An evaluation set that does not represent the true query distribution will produce misleading scores.
How It Works: The Six Dimensions

Completeness measures the fraction of records where key fields used by the agent are populated. For an agent performing customer support, key fields might include account status, product version, and contract tier. A field missing in more than 10% of records that the agent will cite is a conditional finding; missing in more than 25% of records is a blocking finding because the agent will frequently encounter gaps and must either confabulate or escalate, neither of which is acceptable at scale.

Freshness is the maximum acceptable lag between a real-world event and the agent's view of it. For an inventory management agent, a 6-hour lag may cause the agent to recommend stock that has already been depleted. The freshness requirement is set by the use case, not by technical default: agents handling time-sensitive decisions require near-real-time data (sub-1-hour), while agents synthesizing long-term historical trends can tolerate batch refresh.

Schema stability measures the rate of breaking changes to the data source schema. An agent's prompt templates, tool definitions, and retrieval queries are tightly coupled to field names and table structure. Frequent breaking schema changes require frequent agent updates, increasing maintenance cost and outage risk. Sources with more than 2 breaking changes per year are conditional findings; sources without schema versioning policies are blocking.

The agent's service account must have approved read access to each data source. This requires IAM review, data classification check (does the source contain PII, PHI, or trade secrets?), DLP control verification, and approval from the data owner. Any source without approved access is a blocking finding — no workaround exists because agents cannot access unapproved data.

Grounding quality is measured by running a representative evaluation set of 50–100 queries through a prototype RAG pipeline and scoring retrieval precision (fraction of retrieved chunks that are relevant) and recall (fraction of relevant chunks that are retrieved). A precision below 70% is a conditional finding — the agent will frequently retrieve irrelevant context that increases noise and hallucination risk. Below 50% is blocking.

The assessment runs in three phases. In Phase 1 (Data Inventory, days 1–3), the team catalogs all data sources the agent will consume, documents their ownership, and gathers schema documentation. In Phase 2 (Automated Profiling, days 4–8), profiling scripts compute completeness rates, freshness lag, and schema change history from the actual data. In Phase 3 (Grounding Evaluation, days 9–14), a prototype embedding pipeline ingests each source, and a human-labeled evaluation set is used to score retrieval quality. The final assessment report assigns a readiness level and remediation plan for each finding.

Data readiness assessment is performed at the end of the discovery phase, before the architecture design phase begins. This timing ensures that the agent's design accounts for actual data constraints rather than ideal data assumptions. A lightweight rapid assessment (covering only blocking criteria) is appropriate for prototypes; the full assessment is required before production builds.

from typing import List
import json

def evaluate_rag_grounding(
    eval_set: List[dict],  # [{"query": ..., "relevant_doc_ids": [...]}, ...]
    retrieval_fn,          # callable: query -> [{"doc_id": ..., "text": ...}]
    top_k: int = 5,
) -> dict:
    """
    Compute retrieval precision and recall over an evaluation set.
    Each eval item has a query and a list of relevant document IDs.
    """
    precision_scores = []
    recall_scores = []

    for item in eval_set:
        retrieved = retrieval_fn(item["query"])[:top_k]
        retrieved_ids = {r["doc_id"] for r in retrieved}
        relevant_ids = set(item["relevant_doc_ids"])

        if not retrieved_ids:
            precision_scores.append(0.0)
            recall_scores.append(0.0)
            continue

        tp = len(retrieved_ids & relevant_ids)
        precision_scores.append(tp / len(retrieved_ids))
        recall_scores.append(tp / len(relevant_ids) if relevant_ids else 0.0)

    avg_precision = sum(precision_scores) / len(precision_scores)
    avg_recall = sum(recall_scores) / len(recall_scores)
    f1 = 2 * avg_precision * avg_recall / (avg_precision + avg_recall + 1e-9)

    if avg_precision >= 0.80:
        status = "READY"
    elif avg_precision >= 0.70:
        status = "CONDITIONAL"
    else:
        status = "BLOCKING"

    return {
        "precision": round(avg_precision, 3),
        "recall": round(avg_recall, 3),
        "f1": round(f1, 3),
        "n_queries": len(eval_set),
        "top_k": top_k,
        "status": status,
    }


# Example: load a 75-query evaluation set and run against the prototype retriever
with open("data/grounding-eval-set.json") as f:
    eval_set = json.load(f)

result = evaluate_rag_grounding(eval_set, retrieval_fn=prototype_retriever, top_k=5)
print(json.dumps(result, indent=2))

A retail chain evaluating an agentic product recommendation system assessed 7 data sources in 12 days. Three sources received blocking findings: the product catalog had stale images stored as binary blobs that produced zero-information embeddings; the inventory system required an integration that had not been designed; and the customer review database had not been cleared for PII access by the data governance team. Two sources received conditional findings: the purchase history table had a 12-hour lag (acceptable with a remediation plan to add delta processing), and the product description source had only 61% grounding precision due to highly abbreviated product codes.

The assessment report pushed the project start date back by 3 weeks while blocking issues were resolved, but prevented a far more costly discovery during system integration testing. The grounding quality findings reshaped the RAG architecture — the team added a product code expansion step that mapped abbreviations to full descriptions before embedding, raising precision to 84%.
Assessing data quality in isolation from the agent's retrieval design:
High completeness in a table does not help if the data is stored in a format that embeds poorly. Assessment must include a grounding quality measurement.

Grounding quality scores are only meaningful if the evaluation queries reflect the actual distribution of questions the agent will face. Building the eval set from easy, well-formed queries overstates readiness.
Treating access permissions as a low-priority item:

IAM reviews and data governance approvals often involve multiple stakeholders and can take 4–8 weeks. Starting this process last — after all technical work is complete — causes the most visible delays.

Data quality can degrade after deployment. Monthly spot-checks of completeness and freshness metrics, and quarterly grounding quality re-evaluations, are necessary for maintaining production reliability.
Start with access permissions assessment on day 1 of the discovery phase, since approval lead times are the longest-pole item.
Build the grounding evaluation set collaboratively with domain experts who understand what a good retrieval result looks like for each query type.
Profile data from the production environment, not a development copy. Staging databases often have better data quality than production — a common source of optimistic assessment results.
Document each data source's update cadence and schema change process in the assessment report so the operations team knows exactly how to maintain data quality post-deployment.
Assign a data owner for each source who is accountable for maintaining the source above the readiness thresholds after go-live. Without named ownership, data quality degrades silently.

At scale, agents consume data from dozens of sources. Running a full assessment for every source is impractical; instead, sources are tiered by risk. High-risk sources (those where incorrect data would cause material harm or compliance issues) receive the full six-dimension assessment. Medium-risk sources receive completeness, freshness, and access checks only. Low-risk sources (public reference data) receive access verification only. The tiering criteria are defined once and applied across all agent programs in the organization.

Data profiling tools (Great Expectations, Deequ, dbt tests) automate completeness and freshness checks and can be integrated into CI/CD pipelines for ongoing monitoring. Data catalog tools (DataHub, Alation, Collibra) provide schema lineage and change history documentation. For grounding quality evaluation, RAGAS is an open-source framework that automates recall and precision scoring against reference answers using an LLM as judge. Embedding quality tools like Galileo or TruLens can flag semantically poor chunks before they enter the production index.

The access permissions dimension is the security cornerstone of the assessment. Agents must only access data through explicitly approved service accounts with least-privilege IAM policies. PII and regulated data (PHI, financial records) require data processing agreements and, in some jurisdictions, explicit DPA registration before an AI system may process them. The assessment report should list each data source's classification, the specific fields containing regulated data, and the approved processing purpose. This documentation is required evidence for AI compliance audits under frameworks such as the EU AI Act and NIST AI RMF.

Think of a data readiness assessment like a pre-flight checklist for a pilot. The pilot does not take off if any item on the blocking list fails — even if 95% of the checklist passed. A single unapproved data access, or a grounding quality score that falls below the safety threshold, is the aviation equivalent of an unresolved engine warning light. Conditional items are like amber warnings that can be acknowledged with a documented mitigation plan; blocking items are red warnings that prevent departure.
Data readiness assessment covers six dimensions: completeness, freshness, schema stability, access permissions, grounding quality, and lineage. Each must be assessed independently.
Blocking findings halt development until resolved; conditional findings have documented remediation plans with named owners and due dates.
Grounding quality is distinct from data quality — a complete, fresh, well-accessed source can still produce poor retrieval results due to embedding-unfriendly formats or schema design.
Start access permission reviews on day 1 of discovery; IAM and governance approvals have the longest lead times of any assessment activity.
Assessment findings directly shape RAG architecture decisions: chunking strategy, refresh cadence, preprocessing steps, and metadata filter design.
Assign a named data owner for each source who is accountable for maintaining readiness scores post-deployment.
